schools.summary: Gill: 'consenting' means consent with pleasure and delight (Saul well pleased, his heart rejoicing), recorded to magnify his later grace (the persecutor made the apostle); the great persecution scattering the church through Judea and Samaria, only the apostles remaining (courage, duty, or providence at the post of danger); the providence overruling persecution — the scattering spreading the gospel (Acts 1:8; Psalm 76:10), the seed of wider harvest.

Gill: the 'devout men' Christian men eminent in piety, with courage amid the persecution to honor the martyr's body (preparing and burying it, burying the dead an act of kindness and piety); the courage required (risking being marked as his fellow); 'great lamentation' the fitting mourning of the godly dead (not without hope, but the proper grief of love); the church honoring its first martyr, the love of the saints against the fury of the foes.

themes: devout men carried Stephen to his burial

schools.summary: Barnes: 'made havock' (elymaineto) a word for wild beasts (lions, wolves) ravaging — Saul raged against the church like a wild beast, a strong expression of his fury; 'entering into every house' searching out Christians, 'haling' (dragging) men and women to prison without regard to sex; the depth of Saul's sin (the chief of sinners, 1 Timothy 1:15; Acts 22:4) the dark foil to the grace that would convert the persecutor into the apostle, the church suffering in its youth.

themes: Saul made havock of the church
haling men and women to prison
the depth of sin grace would conquer

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: 'out of the eater came forth meat' (Samson's riddle, Judges 14:14) — the persecution designed to destroy the church overruled by God to enlarge it; the enemies meant to put out the fire by scattering, but spread it; the scattered believers (not the apostles, who remained, but the ordinary disciples) went everywhere preaching, undeterred — the providence overruling persecution (Psalm 76:10; Acts 1:8), the gospel spread by the whole church, every believer a witness.

Lightfoot: this Philip the second of the seven deacons (Acts 6:5), not the apostle, one of the scattered now evangelizing; the gospel crossing the old barrier to the despised Samaritans ('the Jews have no dealings with the Samaritans,' John 4:9), breaking down the middle wall (Ephesians 2:14), advancing as Christ foretold (Acts 1:8); 'preached Christ' the substance (1 Corinthians 1:23), meeting the Samaritans' Messianic hope (John 4:25), reaping where Christ had sown (John 4).

Clarke: Philip fixed their attention by both the gravity of his preaching and the miracles; Robertson: the imperfect 'gave heed' — they kept holding their minds on his words, spell-bound; the people 'with one accord' eagerly heeding, drawn by both the word (to the ear) and the works (to the eye); the miracles confirming the message as divine (the signs the messenger's credentials), the eager attentive hearing preparing for faith — the good soil receiving the seed.

themes: the word joined to the works

schools.summary: Clarke presses the reality of demonic possession — the unclean spirits here distinguished from the palsied and lame, so not a name for diseases but real demons (the NT writers meaning real diabolic possessions, denying it 'absolute trifling'); the separate mention proving the demons real; Christ's power over the demonic (the spirits coming out 'crying with loud voice' in rage and defeat) and over disease, the comprehensive deliverance from Satan's bondage and bodily affliction (1 John 3:8).

debates.question: Are the 'unclean spirits' real demons or merely a name for diseases?

sides.position: Real demons — distinguished here from the palsied and lame; the NT means real possessions

themes: unclean spirits came out of many

schools.summary: Barnes: the joy arose from the healing of the afflicted, the conversion of souls, and the mutual joy of families — the tendency of true religion (and of a revival) to produce great joy; Clarke: no wonder, hearing such glorious truths and subjects of such miracles; the gospel bringing gladness (the kingdom 'joy in the Holy Ghost,' Romans 14:17), the joy of salvation no deception can give; the contrast with Samaria's former bondage under Simon's sorcery, the whole city gladdened.

themes: the gladdening power of the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: Simon a Samaritan of Gitton, a wizard; Lightfoot discusses his hold on the people; Simon had long 'bewitched the people of Samaria' by sorcery (holding them in fascination) and 'gave out that himself was some great one' (self-exaltation) — the mark of the deceiver, pointing men to himself not God (the opposite of Philip preaching Christ, v.5); the great rival to the gospel in Samaria, the false miracle-worker to be overcome by the true power of the gospel.

themes: giving out himself was some great one

schools.summary: Clarke: a textual reading adds 'called,' so 'that power of God which is called the Great' — the people ascribing to Simon a divine power (not God himself, but endued with great supernatural power); 'they all gave heed, from the least to the greatest' — the deception universal, embracing the whole city; the depth of a people without the gospel, deceived by false signs (a warning, Matthew 24:24); the false power to be eclipsed by the true gospel (v.12), the deceiver's hold broken.

themes: they all gave heed from the least to the greatest
this man is the great power of God
false power to be overcome by the true

schools.summary: Ellicott: the long time during which the evil fascination had been exercised; Robertson: the same word 'had regard' as 'gave heed'; Simon's hold long-established (not recent), the people deeply and habitually deceived by his sorceries over a long period; the difficulty of overcoming settled error (rooted in habit and time), making the gospel's triumph the more remarkable (v.12) — the deeper the darkness, the brighter the light dispelling it; the warning that men may be long and deeply held by error.

themes: of long time he had bewitched them

schools.summary: Gill: though long attached to Simon, the people believed Philip when he preached Christ — such the power of his ministry and the efficacy of divine grace; Clarke: Philip's word came with greater power than Simon's; they believed 'the things concerning the kingdom of God, and the name of Jesus Christ' (the true object of faith) and were baptized, 'both men and women' (the gospel embracing all); the gospel breaking the long deception, the order — faith then baptism, the city turned from sorcery to Christ.

themes: they believed Philip preaching the kingdom

schools.summary: Gill: Simon's faith historical and temporary (believing Jesus the Messiah, a notional assent, or pretended), not saving; Lightfoot: he believed Jesus the Messiah and so was capable of baptism, but no more; the marks against saving faith — later 'in the gall of bitterness' (v.23), no part in the matter; the difference between professed/notional and saving faith (2 Timothy 3:5), Simon the baptized hypocrite within the church; his 'wondering' the magician's amazement at a greater power, not the converted heart.

sides.position: No — a historical, temporary, or feigned faith (notional assent), shown unconverted by vv.18-23

schools.summary: Lightfoot (with Epiphanius): Philip, a deacon, could preach and baptize but not confer the gift of the Holy Ghost (the extraordinary gifts) by laying on hands — the apostles' peculiar prerogative; so the Jerusalem apostles, hearing Samaria had received the word, sent Peter and John (the two leading apostles) to confer the Spirit; the apostolic confirmation authenticating the Samaritan mission, joining the new converts to the one church, maintaining its unity across the old Jewish-Samaritan barrier.

themes: the apostles heard Samaria had received the word
the unity of the church maintained

schools.summary: Barnes presses the key distinction — the 'Holy Ghost' here not the saving, renewing grace (the Samaritans had this already, believing, vv.6,12) but the extraordinary gifts (tongues, prophecy, miraculous powers) conferred by the apostles' hands in that age; so not a pattern for a delayed saving Spirit or 'second blessing' but the special endowments; the apostles 'prayed for them' (not presuming to confer by their own power) — the gift God's to give, sought by prayer, the apostles his instruments, dependence on God.

debates.question: What is the 'Holy Ghost' the Samaritans received here?

sides.position: The extraordinary gifts (tongues, prophecy, miracles), distinct from the saving grace they already had as believers

themes: that they might receive the Holy Ghost

schools.summary: Gill: the Samaritans had received the Spirit in his saving work (illumination, sanctification, conversion, faith — regenerated, enlightened, baptized believers), but not yet the extraordinary gift (ministry gifts, tongues, miracles); 'baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus' (valid baptism, real faith); the verse does not mean they were unconverted or the saving Spirit withheld — the gift not given was the extraordinary endowment; the providence ordering it at the apostles' hands to confirm the mission and unite the church.

themes: as yet he was fallen upon none of them
baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus
truly converted but not yet endued
saving grace distinct from the gift

schools.summary: Gill: the apostles laid hands not on all but on some directed by the Spirit, designed for the ministry, with ministerial and extraordinary gifts, for the supply and confirmation of the new church; the extraordinary gift conferred through the apostles' hands (their peculiar prerogative, v.14, the means in that age, God the giver in answer to prayer); the gifts for the establishment of the church; the careful reading — peculiar to the apostolic age, not a universal pattern for an ordinary 'confirmation' conferring the saving Spirit (already possessed).

themes: laid they their hands on them
the apostolic means of the gift

schools.summary: Gill: Simon's seeing implies the gift was visible and external (the miraculous effects); Barnes: he saw the power of speaking miraculously and, valuing it for his influence and gain, 'offered them money' (as men of his craft, to make money); the offer revealing his unregenerate, covetous, ambitious heart (the hypocrite of v.13 exposed); the origin of 'simony' (buying/selling spiritual things), Simon the type; the gross error that God's gift could be bought, contrasting the apostles who prayed for it.

themes: Simon saw the Holy Ghost was given

schools.summary: Gill: Simon asked not to receive the gift but to have the power of conferring it on others; Lightfoot: a double error — that the gifts could be bought, and (more) that the power of conferring them could be so attained (even the apostles could not enable another to confer them); Simon's ambition (power and glory, to be again 'some great one,' v.9), not grace; the impious reduction of God's holy gifts to merchandise and tools of self-advancement, the presumptuous offer the occasion of Peter's rebuke (vv.20-23).

themes: that on whomsoever I lay hands
seeking to buy the power of the Spirit

schools.summary: Barnes: 'thy money perish with thee' an expression of Peter's horror and indignation (not properly an imprecation) — 'let your money go to destruction, we abhor your impious offer'; yet also a warning that Simon himself was hastening to ruin; the rebuke of the impious thought that 'the gift of God' could be bought — the gift freely given by grace, not for sale (Isaiah 55:1), to think it purchasable profaning grace; the sternness fitting the sin, the warning of ruin a spur to repentance (v.22).

themes: the gift of God purchased with money

schools.summary: Gill: 'this matter' the gift of the Holy Ghost and the gospel's blessings (forgiveness, righteousness, eternal life), Simon having no part or lot (a Jewish phrase for 'no part in the world to come'); Simon, though baptized and professing (v.13), excluded — his profession empty; the ground 'thy heart is not right in the sight of God' (the necessity of a renewed, sincere heart, 1 Samuel 16:7); the hypocrite unmasked by his offer, God's judgment penetrating to the heart, the call to self-examination.

themes: neither part nor lot in this matter
thy heart is not right with God
the necessity of a right heart

schools.summary: Barnes: Simon an unconverted sinner, the command to repent given to him as such, required actually to repent (not merely wait or seek); Peter, even after the rebuke, calls Simon to repent of his wickedness and pray for forgiveness — the door of mercy held open even to so great a sinner; the 'if perhaps' marking the greatness of the sin and the doubt of his repentance (not God's reluctance to the penitent); the balance of conviction and invitation, repentance and prayer the way to pardon.

themes: mercy offered to the great sinner
repentance and prayer the way to pardon

schools.summary: Gill: 'in the gall of bitterness' = a state of nature and unregeneracy, under covetousness, ambition, hypocrisy, the bitter root of sin (Deuteronomy 29:18; Hebrews 12:15), not grace; 'the bond of iniquity' = bound and captive by sin; Barnes: one single sin may show the absence of true piety as certainly as many, Simon's offer revealing his whole state; Peter's discernment (by the Spirit and the act) reading the heart through the deed; the bondage of the unregenerate to sin (John 8:34), from which only grace frees.

Barnes: Simon directed to pray for himself (v.22) but had no disposition to (sinners often ask others' prayers when too proud or in love with sin to pray themselves); his concern only that the judgments 'come not upon me' — fear of consequences, not sorrow for sin; the difference between fear and repentance (true repentance sorrows for and turns from sin); the witnesses read Simon's response as no true repentance (his later history as a heresiarch confirming), the alarmed but unrepentant sinner.

sides.position: No — he feared the judgment, not the sin; asked others to pray rather than praying himself; an unhumbled, unrepentant heart

themes: pray ye to the Lord for me
that none of these things come upon me

schools.summary: Barnes: the apostles testified and confirmed the church, then on their return 'travelled more at leisure, and preached in the villages also' (a good example for ministers), returning to Jerusalem to bear witness to the resurrection where Christ was crucified; the Samaritan mission extended through many villages; the diligence of using every opportunity and journey to spread the gospel; the old Jewish-Samaritan enmity overcome (Peter and John, cf. Luke 9:54, now preaching to many Samaritans).

themes: preached in many villages of the Samaritans

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the providence in the route ('from Samaria toward the south... desert,' safe from Jewish attack, the place for the appointed meeting), and Philip's prompt obedience ('he did not ask, Wherefore? but arose and went'); the angel sending Philip from the success in Samaria to a desert road without explanation; obedience without demanding the reason (the obedience of faith), the readiness to leave a fruitful field at God's call — God's leading, however strange, purposeful (toward the eunuch).

themes: the angel said, go toward the south

schools.summary: Chrysostom's high praise: this man, in distant Ethiopia, burdened with business, in a superstitious land, came to Jerusalem to worship (not even at a festival), diligent in reading the Scriptures; Lightfoot on Candace (a title of the Ethiopian queens) and the eunuch's status; a great official (treasurer) yet a devout seeker (proselyte or God-fearer), his rank no hindrance to devotion; the providence preparing the seeking soul (drawing him to worship and the word) met by the sent evangelist, the gospel reaching toward the nations (Psalm 68:31).

themes: an eunuch of great authority under Candace

schools.summary: Gill: remarkable that, though in Jerusalem where Jesus was crucified and the apostles preached, the eunuch was not converted there — his conversion ordered for another time, place, and instrument (God's sovereignty in the means and timing); Barnes: he read the Septuagint; the eunuch diligently reading Isaiah in his chariot (redeeming his travel-time, searching even what he could not yet understand, v.31); the providence ordering both the meeting and the very text (Isaiah 53) — the seeking soul searching the Scriptures, prepared for the gospel.

themes: sitting in his chariot read Esaias
the seeking soul searching the Scriptures
providence preparing the meeting and the text

schools.summary: Gill: the Holy Spirit spoke to Philip by an articulate voice or a secret inward impulse; Barnes: the thought in Philip's mind traced to the Spirit's suggestion (all good thoughts and designs from the Spirit), 'join thyself' = go and sit with him; the divine guidance of the whole encounter (the angel to the road, v.26, the Spirit to the chariot) bringing the prepared soul and the directed evangelist together; the Spirit's leading essential to fruitful ministry, to be heeded and followed.

themes: the Spirit the source of good impulses

schools.summary: Barnes: 'Philip ran' indicating his haste and eagerness to obey the Spirit (though difficulties might have been raised — the eunuch a stranger, of rank, reading — he obeyed without hesitation, following the first suggestions of the Spirit); Philip heard him read Isaiah aloud (his opening), and asked the wise, gentle question 'Understandest thou what thou readest?' (meeting the eunuch where he was, drawing out his need); the skillful, tactful approach of the soul-winner, the Spirit's leading and human wisdom together.

themes: the wise approach to the soul

schools.summary: Gill commends the eunuch's uncommon modesty — not haughty as great men are apt to be, but confessing his ignorance and the need of those appointed by God to open the Scriptures; a man of rank humbly owning he cannot understand without a guide (the teachable spirit, Psalm 25:9); the Scriptures needing to be opened and explained (the ministry necessary, Luke 24:45); he invited Philip to sit and teach (welcoming the word); the seeking soul's humility rewarded with the guide God sent.

themes: how can I, except some man guide me
come up and sit with him
the need of a guide to the Scriptures

schools.summary: Gill: the Scripture already divided into sections, the text given per the Septuagint (and whether the eunuch used it); Lightfoot raises the eunuch's status (Jew, proselyte, or Gentile) and the version; the eunuch reading Isaiah 53:7 (the 'gospel in the Old Testament'), the suffering Servant led 'as a sheep to the slaughter,' silent 'like a lamb dumb before his shearer' (fulfilled in Christ's trials, Matthew 27:12-14); the providence ordering the very text of the suffering Christ for the seeker's conversion.

themes: he was led as a sheep to the slaughter
like a lamb dumb before his shearer

schools.summary: Gill: 'his humiliation' = Christ's low estate (human nature, mean parentage, sorrows, apprehended, scourged, condemned, mocked, crucified), 'his judgment taken away' = justice denied him (condemned without cause, the innocent treated as guilty); 'who shall declare his generation?' variously read (the wickedness of his generation, his endless/eternal life, or his spiritual offspring); 'his life taken from the earth' = cut off in death (Daniel 9:26); the depth of Christ's humiliation as the suffering Servant, the door to the gospel for the eunuch.

debates.question: What does 'who shall declare his generation?' mean?

sides.position: The wickedness of the generation that condemned him (none can declare it)
His endless/eternal life or his spiritual offspring (who can declare/number them)

themes: in his humiliation his judgment was taken away
his life is taken from the earth

schools.summary: Barnes: a natural inquiry, for nothing in the text itself determined expressly to whom it referred; the eunuch's honest perplexity (of himself or another?) the genuine question of a seeking soul; the ancient Jews applied the passage to the Messiah (the Targum on Isaiah 52:13), though the eunuch may not have known; the teachable spirit (v.31) seeking the meaning, his honest question the very door for Philip to preach Jesus (v.35) — the seeking soul that asks honestly answered (Matthew 7:7).

themes: of whom speaketh the prophet this
of himself, or of some other man
the open door for the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: 'opened his mouth' a phrase (frequent in Jewish writings) for solemn, free, clear teaching of Scripture; Barnes: Philip, taking Isaiah 53 as his text, showed that Jesus of Nazareth exactly answered the prophet's description; he 'began at the same scripture' (meeting the seeker where he was) and 'preached unto him Jesus' — the suffering Servant identified as the crucified Christ (the prophecy fulfilled, Psalm 40:7); preaching Christ from the Scriptures, Jesus the substance of the gospel.

themes: the suffering Servant the crucified Christ

schools.summary: Gill: as they traveled (Philip preaching, the eunuch hearing and believing) they came to water, and the eunuch, instructed about Christ and baptism, requested it; Barnes: the text does not specify river, brook, or pool; the eunuch's eager request ('what doth hinder me?') the fruit of faith seeking the ordinance — the proper order (faith then baptism), the new believer eager to confess Christ openly and obey at once; Philip's preaching of Jesus including his ordinances.

themes: what doth hinder me to be baptized

schools.summary: Gill: faith in Christ and its profession necessary prerequisites to baptism — not a historical, temporary, or feigned faith (like Simon's, v.13) but believing with the heart unto righteousness (Romans 10:10); Philip required a heartfelt faith, the eunuch confessed 'Jesus Christ is the Son of God' (the good confession, 1 Timothy 6:12); the verse absent from many of the best/ancient MSS (likely an early addition, omitted by many editors), yet the principle (faith confessed before baptism) sound and scriptural.

debates.question: Is Acts 8:37 part of the original text?

sides.position: Absent from many of the best/ancient MSS, likely an early addition (omitted by many editors), though the principle (faith before baptism) is sound

themes: if thou believest with all thine heart
I believe that Jesus Christ is the Son of God

schools.summary: Gill (a Baptist): 'they went down both into the water' (and 'came up out of,' v.39) evidence that baptism was by immersion; Barnes cautions the preposition 'into' (eis) need not mean entrance ('to/unto the water'), not deciding the mode; the long debate between immersionists and those allowing pouring/sprinkling, the prepositions cited both ways and not decisive; the certain point — the eunuch baptized upon his confession, received by the appointed ordinance — the mode debated, the ordinance and its meaning agreed.

debates.question: Does Acts 8:38 prove baptism by immersion?

sides.position: Yes — 'went down into' and 'came up out of' the water indicate immersion
Not decisive — the preposition 'into' (eis) may mean 'to/unto the water,' not determining the mode

themes: they went down both into the water

schools.summary: Gill: 'come up out of the water' again taken (with v.38) as evidence of immersion; Barnes: the preposition 'out of' (ek) opposed to 'into,' the Spirit who directed Philip (v.29) now catching him away; the miraculous removal (Philip's work done, borne to his next service, v.40, confirming the divine hand to the eunuch); 'he went on his way rejoicing' — the new convert's joy in his salvation (not in the departed teacher but in Christ, Romans 14:17), the firstfruits of Africa going home with the gospel.

themes: the eunuch saw him no more
the joy of the new convert

schools.summary: Barnes: Philip found at Azotus (the Ashdod of the Philistines), preaching through all the cities to Caesarea; Lightfoot calculates the distance (Gaza to Azotus ~34 miles) and traces the coastal route (Azotus, Ashkelon, Jamnia, to Caesarea); the Spirit bearing Philip to continue his work, the diligent evangelist preaching city after city (the gospel spreading along his path); Philip settling at Caesarea, where later found (Acts 21:8, 'Philip the evangelist, one of the seven') — tireless, faithful ministry.

themes: preached in all the cities till Caesarea
the gospel spreading through the cities
